hints: Rule: The pronoun I always takes am — no other form of be works with I.
The subject here is I, so choose the first-person singular form of be.

a1.am: Correct: 'am' is the only form of be used with I. I am happy is a standard first-person statement.
a1.is: 'is' is used with he, she, it — not with I. The subject I requires am.
a1.are: 'are' pairs with you, we, they. The subject I needs am.

hints: Rule: Third-person singular subjects (he, she, it) take is.
The subject is She — a third-person singular pronoun.

a2.am: 'am' is only for I. She requires is.
a2.is: Correct: 'is' is the form of be for she. She is a teacher — identification with be.
a2.are: 'are' is for you, we, they. She is singular and needs is.

hints: Rule: Plural subjects (we, they) and you always take are.
The subject They is third-person plural.

a3.am: 'am' is only for I. They is plural and needs are.
a3.is: 'is' is for singular subjects. They is plural, so is doesn't fit.
a3.are: Correct: 'are' is the plural form of be. They are in the park.

hints: Rule: He is a third-person singular pronoun and takes is.
The subject is He, so pick the singular be form.

a4.am: 'am' is only for I. He needs is.
a4.is: Correct: 'is' is the be form for he/she/it. He is my friend.
a4.are: 'are' is for you/we/they. He is singular.

hints: Rule: We is first-person plural and always takes are.
The subject is We — pick the plural form of be.

a5.am: 'am' is only for I. We is plural and needs are.
a5.is: 'is' is for he/she/it. We is plural, so is doesn't work.
a5.are: Correct: 'are' is for we. We are students.

hints: Rule: It is a third-person singular pronoun and takes is.
It here refers to the weather — an impersonal construction needing is.

a6.am: 'am' is only for I. It takes is.
a6.is: Correct: 'is' pairs with it. It is cold — impersonal be + adjective.
a6.are: 'are' is for you/we/they. It is singular.

hints: Rule: You always takes are, whether singular or plural.
The subject is You — second person.

a7.am: 'am' is only for I. You takes are.
a7.is: 'is' is for he/she/it. You always takes are.
a7.are: Correct: 'are' is the only form of be for you. You are very kind.

hints: Rule: I always pairs with am — the only be form for first person singular.
The subject is I, indicating origin.

a8.am: Correct: 'am' is the be form for I. I am from Ukraine.
a8.is: 'is' is for he/she/it. I requires am.
a8.are: 'are' is for you/we/they. I needs am.

hints: Rule: The cat = it, a third-person singular noun, so use is.
A singular countable noun subject takes is.

a9.am: 'am' is only for I. The cat (= it) takes is.
a9.is: Correct: 'is' goes with a singular noun. The cat is on the sofa.
a9.are: 'are' is for plural subjects. Cat is singular.

hints: Rule: My parents is plural (= they), so it takes are.
Parents is a plural noun requiring the plural form of be.

a10.am: 'am' is only for I. Parents is plural — use are.
a10.is: 'is' is for singular. Parents is plural, so is doesn't fit.
a10.are: Correct: 'are' is the plural be. My parents are at home.

hints: Rule: I always takes am regardless of the rest of the sentence.
Subject I + adjective tired needs am.

a11.am: Correct: 'am' — the only be for I. I am tired.
a11.is: 'is' is for he/she/it. I requires am.
a11.are: 'are' is for you/we/they. I needs am.

hints: Rule: This is a singular demonstrative (= it), so use is.
The subject This points to one item.

a12.am: 'am' is only for I. This = it, needs is.
a12.is: Correct: 'is' pairs with this (singular). This is my book.
a12.are: 'are' is for plural. This is singular.

hints: Rule: Children is the irregular plural of child (= they), so use are.
The noun children is plural.

a13.am: 'am' is only for I. Children is plural — use are.
a13.is: 'is' is for singular. Children is plural.
a13.are: Correct: 'are' pairs with plural subjects. The children are in the garden.

hints: Rule: I exclusively takes am.
I ____ a student — classic identity sentence with I.

a14.am: Correct: 'am' — the only be for I. I am a student.
a14.is: 'is' is for he/she/it. I requires am.
a14.are: 'are' is for you/we/they. I needs am.

hints: Rule: The dog is singular (= it), so use is.
A singular noun with the definite article takes is.

a15.am: 'am' is only for I. The dog = it, needs is.
a15.is: Correct: 'is' pairs with singular. The dog is big.
a15.are: 'are' is for plural. Dog is singular.

hints: Rule: Two subjects joined by and form a plural compound subject (= they).
Tom and Jerry = two people, so use are.

a16.am: 'am' is only for I. Compound subject = they, needs are.
a16.is: 'is' is for singular. Two subjects with and = plural.
a16.are: Correct: 'are' — compound subjects with and take are. Tom and Jerry are friends.

hints: Rule: My name is singular (= it), so use is.
The subject My name is a singular noun phrase.

a17.am: 'am' is only for I. My name = it, needs is.
a17.is: Correct: 'is' pairs with singular. My name is Anna.
a17.are: 'are' is for plural. My name is singular.

hints: Rule: We is first-person plural and takes are.
The subject We with adjective hungry needs plural be.

a18.am: 'am' is only for I. We is plural — use are.
a18.is: 'is' is for he/she/it. We is plural.
a18.are: Correct: 'are' pairs with we. We are hungry.

hints: Rule: She is third-person singular and takes is, even in negatives (is not).
The subject She keeps is even with not following.

a19.am: 'am' is only for I. She requires is.
a19.is: Correct: 'is' — She is not at school. Is stays in negatives.
a19.are: 'are' is for you/we/they. She is singular.

hints: Rule: I takes am even in negative sentences (am not).
The subject I keeps am regardless of negation.

a20.am: Correct: 'am' — I am not ready. Am stays in negatives with I.
a20.is: 'is' is for he/she/it. I needs am.
a20.are: 'are' is for you/we/they. I needs am.

hints: Rule: In questions with you, are moves before the subject: Are you ...?
You takes are in both statements and questions.

a21.Am: 'Am' is only for I (Am I...?). You needs Are.
a21.Is: 'Is' is for he/she/it. You requires Are.
a21.Are: Correct: 'Are' — Are you a doctor? Standard inversion with you.

hints: Rule: Flowers is a plural noun (= they), so use are.
The plural form flowers requires the plural be.

a22.am: 'am' is only for I. Flowers is plural — use are.
a22.is: 'is' is for singular. Flowers is plural.

hints: Rule: In There is/are, be agrees with the noun that follows. A book is singular.
After there, the noun a book is singular, so use is.

a23.am: 'am' is only for I. There + be uses is or are.
a23.is: Correct: 'is' — a book is singular. There is a book on the desk.
a23.are: 'are' is for plural nouns. A book is singular.

hints: Rule: There is/are — many people is plural, so use are.
Many + people signals a plural noun requiring are.

a24.am: 'am' is only for I. There + be doesn't use am.
a24.is: 'is' is for singular. Many people is plural.
a24.are: Correct: 'are' — There are many people in the room.

hints: Rule: He takes is even in negatives (is not / isn't).
The subject He requires is; not doesn't change the be form.

a25.am: 'am' is only for I. He needs is.
a25.is: Correct: 'is' — He is not happy. Is stays for he in negatives.
a25.are: 'are' is for you/we/they. He is singular.

hints: Rule: In questions with it, is comes first: Is it ...?
It is third-person singular, so Is is correct.

a26.Am: 'Am' is only for I. It needs Is.
a26.Is: Correct: 'Is' — Is it your pen? Standard inversion with it.
a26.Are: 'Are' is for you/we/they. It is singular.

hints: Rule: Brothers is plural (= they), so use are.
The noun brothers is plural.

a27.am: 'am' is only for I. Brothers is plural — use are.
a27.is: 'is' is for singular. Brothers is plural.

hints: Rule: Weather is an uncountable singular noun (= it), so use is.
Weather always behaves as singular.

a28.am: 'am' is only for I. Weather = it, needs is.
a28.is: Correct: 'is' — The weather is nice today.
a28.are: 'are' is for plural. Weather is uncountable singular.

hints: Rule: We takes are even in negative sentences (are not / aren't).
The subject We is first-person plural.

a29.am: 'am' is only for I. We takes are.
a29.is: 'is' is for he/she/it. We is plural.
a29.are: Correct: 'are' — We are not from this city.

hints: Rule: In questions with I, am comes first: Am I ...?
I takes Am in question inversion.

a30.Am: Correct: 'Am' — Am I late? Standard inversion with I.
a30.Is: 'Is' is for he/she/it. I needs Am.
a30.Are: 'Are' is for you/we/they. I needs Am.

hints: Rule: There is/are — three cups is plural, so use are.
The numeral three + cups signals plural.

a31.am: 'am' is only for I. There + be uses is or are.
a31.is: 'is' is for singular. Three cups is plural.
a31.are: Correct: 'are' — There are three cups on the table.

hints: Rule: In questions with they, Are comes first.
They is third-person plural and takes are.

a32.Am: 'Am' is only for I. They needs Are.
a32.Is: 'Is' is for singular. They is plural.

hints: Rule: I takes am even in negatives (am not).
I ____ not sure — the subject I requires am.

a33.am: Correct: 'am' — I am not sure. Am stays for I in negatives.
a33.is: 'is' is for he/she/it. I needs am.
a33.are: 'are' is for you/we/they. I needs am.

hints: Rule: This exercise = it, singular, so use is.
The demonstrative This points to one item.

a34.am: 'am' is only for I. This exercise = it, needs is.
a34.is: Correct: 'is' — This exercise is not very difficult.
a34.are: 'are' is for plural. This exercise is singular.

hints: Rule: These shoes — plural demonstrative + plural noun (= they), so are.
These (plural of this) + shoes (plural) requires are.

a35.am: 'am' is only for I. These shoes is plural — use are.
a35.is: 'is' is for singular. These shoes is plural.
a35.are: Correct: 'are' — These shoes are too small for me.

hints: Rule: Milk is uncountable singular (= it), so use is.
Milk always behaves as a singular noun.

a36.am: 'am' is only for I. Milk = it, needs is.
a36.is: Correct: 'is' — The milk is in the fridge.
a36.are: 'are' is for plural. Milk is uncountable singular.

hints: Rule: She is third-person singular, even inside a subordinate clause.
In the clause after 'think', the subject she takes is.

a37.am: 'am' is only for I. She needs is.
a37.is: Correct: 'is' — I think she is right.
a37.are: 'are' is for you/we/they. She is singular.

hints: Rule: Collective noun team, when acting as a single unit, usually takes is.
The team here acts as one group (ready together), so is is most natural.

a38.am: 'am' is only for I. Team is third person.
a38.is: Correct: 'is' — the team as a single unit takes is. The team is ready.
a38.are: 'are' is possible when emphasising individual members, but as a unit, is is standard.

hints: Rule: In neither...nor constructions, the verb agrees with the nearest subject.
The nearest subject is the students (plural), so use are.

a39.am: 'am' is only for I. Neither subject is I.
a39.is: 'is' would apply if the nearest subject were singular, but the students is plural.
a39.are: Correct: 'are' — proximity agreement with the students (plural).

hints: Rule: Every + singular noun is always grammatically singular.
Every student = one student at a time, so is.

a40.am: 'am' is only for I. Every student is third person.
a40.is: Correct: 'is' — every + noun = singular. Every student is responsible.
a40.are: 'are' is for plural. Every student is grammatically singular.

hints: Rule: News looks plural but is always grammatically singular (= it).
Despite the -s ending, news is uncountable singular.

a41.am: 'am' is only for I. News = it, needs is.
a41.is: Correct: 'is' — news is always singular. The news is surprising.
a41.are: 'are' would be wrong — news is singular despite its -s ending.

hints: Rule: In There is/are, be agrees with the noun. Information is uncountable singular.
A lot of information = uncountable, so is.

a42.am: 'am' is only for I. There + be uses is or are.
a42.is: Correct: 'is' — information is uncountable singular. There is a lot of information.
a42.are: 'are' is for countable plural. Information is uncountable singular.

hints: Rule: Academic subjects ending in -ics (physics, mathematics) are singular.
Physics as a discipline is singular despite the -s ending.

a43.am: 'am' is only for I. Physics is third person.
a43.is: Correct: 'is' — physics as a subject = singular. Physics is my favourite subject.
a43.are: 'are' is for plural. Physics is grammatically singular.

hints: Rule: Both + plural noun is always plural, so use are.
Both signals a plural subject.

a44.am: 'am' is only for I. Both answers is plural.
a44.is: 'is' is for singular. Both = always plural.

hints: Rule: It is + adjective + that-clause — impersonal construction with formal subject it.
The formal subject It takes is.

a45.am: 'am' is only for I. It takes is.
a45.is: Correct: 'is' — It is important that... Impersonal it + be + adjective.
a45.are: 'are' is for plural. It is singular.

hints: Rule: Each of + plural noun = grammatically singular (each = one at a time).
Each makes the subject singular regardless of the noun after of.

a46.am: 'am' is only for I. Each is singular third person.
a46.is: Correct: 'is' — each of + plural = singular. Each of the rooms is clean.
a46.are: 'are' is for plural. Each is grammatically singular.

hints: Rule: Glasses is a pluralia tantum noun (always plural), so use are.
Glasses (eyewear) has no singular form.

a47.am: 'am' is only for I. Glasses is plural.
a47.is: 'is' is for singular. Glasses is always plural.
a47.are: Correct: 'are' — glasses is always plural. My glasses are on the table.

hints: Rule: Everybody/Everyone is grammatically singular, so use is.
Despite meaning 'all people', everybody takes a singular verb.

a48.am: 'am' is only for I. Everybody is third person.
a48.is: Correct: 'is' — everybody = singular. Everybody is welcome.
a48.are: 'are' is for plural. Everybody is grammatically singular.

hints: Rule: There is/are — several reasons is plural.
Several + plural noun requires are.

a49.am: 'am' is only for I. There + be uses is or are.
a49.is: 'is' is for singular. Several reasons is plural.

hints: Rule: Furniture is uncountable singular (= it), so use is.
Furniture has no plural form.

a50.am: 'am' is only for I. Furniture = it.
a50.is: Correct: 'is' — furniture is uncountable singular. The furniture is very old.
a50.are: 'are' is for plural. Furniture is uncountable singular.

hints: Rule: One of + plural noun = singular (the head word is one).
One makes the subject singular.

a51.am: 'am' is only for I. One is third-person singular.
a51.is: Correct: 'is' — one of my friends = singular. One of my friends is an engineer.
a51.are: 'are' is for plural. The head word one is singular.

hints: Rule: Police is always treated as plural (= they) in British English.
Police has no singular form and always takes are.

a52.am: 'am' is only for I. Police is plural.
a52.is: 'is' is for singular. Police is always plural in English.
a52.are: Correct: 'are' — police is always plural. The police are looking for the suspect.

hints: Rule: None of + uncountable noun = singular. Homework is uncountable.
Homework has no plural — none of it is treated as singular.

a53.am: 'am' is only for I. None of the homework is singular.
a53.is: Correct: 'is' — homework is uncountable, so none of it = singular.
a53.are: 'are' is for plural. Homework is uncountable singular.

hints: Rule: A number of + plural noun = plural (= many). Don't confuse with the number of.
A number of students means 'several students' — plural.

a54.am: 'am' is only for I. A number of students is plural.
a54.is: 'is' would fit the number of (where number is the head), but a number of = 'several' — plural.
a54.are: Correct: 'are' — a number of students = plural. A number of students are absent.

hints: Rule: The majority of + countable plural noun usually takes a plural verb.
The majority of the students — students is plural.

a55.am: 'am' is only for I. Majority of the students is plural.
a55.is: 'is' could work with uncountable nouns, but students is countable plural.
a55.are: Correct: 'are' — the majority of the students are satisfied.

hints: Rule: The number of + noun = singular (number is the head word).
Don't confuse: the number of = singular (is), a number of = plural (are).

a56.am: 'am' is only for I. The number is singular.
a56.is: Correct: 'is' — the number of applicants = singular. The number is growing.
a56.are: 'are' fits a number of, but the number of = singular.

hints: Rule: In either...or, the verb agrees with the nearest subject.
The nearest subject is his assistants (plural), so use are.

a57.am: 'am' is only for I. Neither subject is I.
a57.is: 'is' would apply if the nearest subject were singular, but assistants is plural.
a57.are: Correct: 'are' — proximity agreement with his assistants (plural).

hints: Rule: In neither...nor, the verb agrees with the nearest subject.
The nearest subject is the professor (singular), so use is.

a58.am: 'am' is only for I. The subjects are students and professor.
a58.is: Correct: 'is' — proximity agreement with the professor (singular).
a58.are: 'are' would apply if the nearest subject were plural, but professor is singular.

hints: Rule: Criteria is the Latin plural of criterion, so use are.
Note: criteria (plural) ≠ criterion (singular).

a59.am: 'am' is only for I. Criteria is plural.
a59.is: 'is' would suit criterion (singular), but criteria is plural.
a59.are: Correct: 'are' — criteria is plural. What are the criteria?

hints: Rule: Academic subjects in -ics (Mathematics, Physics) are grammatically singular.
Mathematics as a discipline is singular despite the -s.

a60.am: 'am' is only for I. Mathematics is third person.
a60.is: Correct: 'is' — mathematics = singular. Mathematics is essential.
a60.are: 'are' is for plural. Mathematics is singular.

hints: Rule: The collective noun committee, as a single body, usually takes is.
Committee acting as one unit takes singular be.

a61.am: 'am' is only for I. Committee is third person.
a61.is: Correct: 'is' — committee as a body = singular. The committee is divided.
a61.are: 'are' is possible for individual members, but as a unit is is standard.

hints: Rule: Half of + singular noun = singular.
Half of the report — report is singular.

a62.am: 'am' is only for I. Half of the report is singular.
a62.is: Correct: 'is' — half of the report = singular.
a62.are: 'are' would fit half of the reports, but here it's singular.

hints: Rule: There is + singular noun. No excuse is singular.
The noun excuse is singular, so is.

a63.am: 'am' is only for I. There + be uses is or are.
a63.are: 'are' is for plural. Excuse is singular.

hints: Rule: In not only...but also, the verb agrees with the nearest subject.
The nearest subject is the quality (singular), so use is.

a64.am: 'am' is only for I. The quality is singular.
a64.is: Correct: 'is' — proximity agreement with the quality (singular).
a64.are: 'are' is for plural. The nearest subject quality is singular.

hints: Rule: Fraction + of + uncountable noun = singular.
Land is uncountable, so two thirds of the land = singular.

a65.am: 'am' is only for I. Two thirds of the land is singular.
a65.is: Correct: 'is' — land is uncountable, so the fraction is singular.
a65.are: 'are' would apply with a countable plural, but land is uncountable.

hints: Rule: In formal/academic English, data is treated as plural (from Latin datum).
In the research context, data = plural, so are.

a66.am: 'am' is only for I. Data is plural in formal usage.
a66.is: 'is' is acceptable informally, but in academic context data is plural.
a66.are: Correct: 'are' — in formal English, data = plural. The data are being analysed.

hints: Rule: Each of + plural noun = singular (each = one at a time).
Each makes the subject singular.

a67.am: 'am' is only for I. Each is singular.
a67.is: Correct: 'is' — each of the candidates = singular.
a67.are: 'are' is for plural. Each is grammatically singular.

hints: Rule: None of + countable plural can take are when the noun is clearly plural.
None of the answers — answers is countable plural.

a68.am: 'am' is only for I. None of the answers is third person.
a68.is: 'is' is formally possible, but with plural countable answers, are is more natural.
a68.are: Correct: 'are' — none of the answers (plural) = are.

hints: Rule: Audience as a single group usually takes is.
The audience shows a unified reaction (enthusiastic).

a69.am: 'am' is only for I. Audience is third person.
a69.is: Correct: 'is' — audience as a unit = singular. The audience is enthusiastic.
a69.are: 'are' is possible for individual members, but as a group is is standard.

hints: Rule: Half of + countable plural = plural.
Half of the cookies — cookies is countable plural.

a70.am: 'am' is only for I. Half of the cookies is plural.
a70.is: 'is' would suit half of + uncountable, but cookies is countable plural.
a70.are: Correct: 'are' — half of the cookies = plural.

hints: Rule: Country names in plural form (The United States) are grammatically singular.
The United States is one country, so is.

a71.am: 'am' is only for I. The United States is singular.
a71.is: Correct: 'is' — The United States = one country = singular.
a71.are: 'are' is for plural. Despite States, the country name is singular.

hints: Rule: Scissors is pluralia tantum (always plural), so use are.
Scissors has no singular form.

a72.am: 'am' is only for I. Scissors is plural.
a72.is: 'is' is for singular. Scissors is always plural.
a72.are: Correct: 'are' — scissors is always plural. The scissors are in the drawer.

hints: Rule: It is + adverb + past participle — formal impersonal construction.
The formal subject It takes is.

a73.am: 'am' is only for I. It takes is.
a73.is: Correct: 'is' — It is widely acknowledged... Formal impersonal it.
a73.are: 'are' is for plural. It is singular.

hints: Rule: In neither...nor, the verb agrees with the nearest subject.
The nearest subject is the testimony (singular), so is.

a74.am: 'am' is only for I. The testimony is singular.
a74.are: 'are' would apply if the nearest subject were plural.

hints: Rule: Phenomena is the Latin plural of phenomenon, so use are.
Phenomena (plural) ≠ phenomenon (singular).

a75.am: 'am' is only for I. Phenomena is plural.
a75.is: 'is' would suit phenomenon (singular), but phenomena is plural.
a75.are: Correct: 'are' — phenomena = plural. The phenomena are extremely rare.

hints: Rule: What is at stake — what here refers to one thing (singular).
What = one abstract thing, so is.

a76.am: 'am' is only for I. What is third person.
a76.is: Correct: 'is' — What is at stake here is... — what = singular.
a76.are: 'are' could work if what referred to multiple things, but here it's one.

hints: Rule: A series of + plural noun = singular (series is the head word).
A series = one series, so is.

a77.am: 'am' is only for I. A series is singular.
a77.is: Correct: 'is' — a series of lectures = singular. A series is scheduled.
a77.are: 'are' is for plural. Series with a = singular.

hints: Rule: In formal English, media is treated as plural (from Latin medium).
Media here refers to multiple outlets, so are.

a78.am: 'am' is only for I. Media is plural in formal usage.
a78.is: 'is' is acceptable informally, but in formal style media is plural.
a78.are: Correct: 'are' — in formal English, media = plural. The media are often criticised.

hints: Rule: Every one of + plural noun = singular (every one = each individual).
Every one makes the subject singular.

a79.am: 'am' is only for I. Every one is singular.
a79.is: Correct: 'is' — every one of the proposals = singular.
a79.are: 'are' is for plural. Every one is grammatically singular.

hints: Rule: Candidates is plural, so the verb in the relative clause is are.
In the clause 'by which candidates ___ judged', the subject is candidates (plural).

a80.am: 'am' is only for I. Candidates is plural.
a80.is: 'is' is for singular. Candidates is plural.
a80.are: Correct: 'are' — candidates = plural. Candidates are judged.

hints: Rule: No fewer than + number + plural noun = plural.
Fifty participants is plural.

a81.am: 'am' is only for I. Participants is plural.
a81.is: 'is' is for singular. Participants is plural.

hints: Rule: There is + uncountable noun. Evidence is uncountable.
Little evidence = uncountable singular, so is.

a82.am: 'am' is only for I. Evidence is uncountable.
a82.are: 'are' is for plural. Evidence is uncountable singular.

hints: Rule: Staff, when emphasising individual members, takes are.
Divided implies separate people, so staff = plural here.

a83.am: 'am' is only for I. Staff is plural here.
a83.is: 'is' would apply if staff acted as a unit, but divided suggests individuals.
a83.are: Correct: 'are' — staff (individuals) = plural. The staff are divided.

hints: Rule: More than one + singular noun = grammatically singular.
Formally, more than one candidate = singular (one candidate).

a84.am: 'am' is only for I. More than one candidate is singular.
a84.is: Correct: 'is' — more than one + singular = singular grammatically.
a84.are: 'are' is semantically logical but grammatically more than one = singular.

hints: Rule: In not only...but also, the verb agrees with the nearest subject.
The nearest subject is the methodology (singular).

a85.am: 'am' is only for I. The methodology is singular.
a85.are: 'are' would apply if the nearest subject were plural.

hints: Rule: The percentage of + noun = singular (percentage is the head word).
Percentage is a singular noun.

a86.am: 'am' is only for I. Percentage is singular.
a86.are: 'are' is for plural. Percentage is singular.

hints: Rule: A pair of + noun = singular (pair is the head word).
A pair = singular despite the plural gloves.

a87.am: 'am' is only for I. A pair is singular.
a87.is: Correct: 'is' — a pair of gloves = singular.
a87.are: 'are' is for plural. Pair is singular.

hints: Rule: A great many + plural noun = plural.
A great many factors = many, so are.

a88.am: 'am' is only for I. A great many factors is plural.
a88.is: 'is' is for singular. A great many factors is plural.
a88.are: Correct: 'are' — a great many factors = plural.

hints: Rule: Acoustics meaning 'sound qualities of a room' is plural.
Acoustics (properties) = plural, so are.

a89.am: 'am' is only for I. Acoustics is plural here.
a89.is: 'is' would apply to acoustics as a discipline, but here it means properties.
a89.are: Correct: 'are' — acoustics (properties) = plural. The acoustics are excellent.

hints: Rule: The amount of + uncountable noun = singular (amount is the head word).
Amount is a singular noun.

a90.am: 'am' is only for I. Amount is singular.
a90.is: Correct: 'is' — the amount of effort = singular.
a90.are: 'are' is for plural. Amount is singular.

hints: Rule: It is + preposition + noun — formal cleft sentence with it.
The formal subject It takes is.

a91.am: 'am' is only for I. It takes is.
a91.is: Correct: 'is' — It is upon this principle... — formal cleft.
a91.are: 'are' is for plural. It is singular.

hints: Rule: Parenthetical phrases (along with...) don't change the subject's number.
The hypothesis = singular; along with data is parenthetical.

a92.am: 'am' is only for I. Hypothesis is singular.
a92.is: Correct: 'is' — along with doesn't add to the subject. Hypothesis = singular.
a92.are: 'are' is wrong because along with is parenthetical; hypothesis alone is the subject.

hints: Rule: These findings is plural, so use are in the whether-clause.
Findings = plural noun.

a93.am: 'am' is only for I. Findings is plural.
a93.is: 'is' is for singular. Findings is plural.
a93.are: Correct: 'are' — these findings = plural. Whether these findings are replicable...

hints: Rule: The crux of the matter = singular (crux is the head word).
Crux is a singular noun.

a94.am: 'am' is only for I. Crux is singular.
a94.is: Correct: 'is' — the crux of the matter = singular.
a94.are: 'are' is for plural. Crux is singular.

hints: Rule: In neither...nor, the verb agrees with the nearest subject.
The nearest subject is the implications (plural).

a95.am: 'am' is only for I. Implications is plural.
a95.is: 'is' would apply if the nearest subject were singular.

hints: Rule: There is + singular noun in formal inverted order.
A collection = singular, so is.

a96.am: 'am' is only for I. A collection is singular.
a96.is: Correct: 'is' — a collection = singular. There is a collection...
a96.are: 'are' is for plural. Collection is singular.

hints: Rule: Dynamics meaning 'the way things interact' is plural.
The dynamics (interactions) = plural.

a97.am: 'am' is only for I. Dynamics is plural here.
a97.is: 'is' would apply to dynamics as a discipline, but here it means interactions.

hints: Rule: It is precisely + noun — emphatic cleft with formal it.
The formal subject It takes is.

a98.am: 'am' is only for I. It takes is.
a98.is: Correct: 'is' — It is precisely this ambiguity... — emphatic cleft.
a98.are: 'are' is for plural. It is singular.

hints: Rule: The onus of proof = singular (onus is the head word).
Onus is a singular noun.

a99.am: 'am' is only for I. Onus is singular.
a99.is: Correct: 'is' — the onus of proof = singular.
a99.are: 'are' is for plural. Onus is singular.

hints: Rule: Analyses is the Latin plural of analysis, so use are.
Analyses (plural) ≠ analysis (singular).

a100.am: 'am' is only for I. Analyses is plural.
a100.is: 'is' would suit analysis (singular), but analyses is plural.
a100.are: Correct: 'are' — analyses = plural. The analyses are inconclusive.

hints: Rule: So + adjective + be + subject — inverted order; be agrees with the subject.
The subject is the impact (singular).

a101.am: 'am' is only for I. Impact is singular.
a101.is: Correct: 'is' — So profound is the impact... Impact is singular.
a101.are: 'are' is for plural. Impact is singular.

hints: Rule: Ramifications is plural, so use are.
The ramifications = plural noun.

a102.am: 'am' is only for I. Ramifications is plural.
a102.is: 'is' is for singular. Ramifications is plural.

hints: Rule: It is not without reason — formal impersonal it with negation.
The formal subject It takes is even with not.

a103.am: 'am' is only for I. It takes is.
a103.is: Correct: 'is' — It is not without reason... — formal impersonal construction.
a103.are: 'are' is for plural. It is singular.

hints: Rule: Among... + be + subject — inverted; be agrees with the subject.
The subject is the lack of transparency (singular).

a104.am: 'am' is only for I. The lack is singular.
a104.is: Correct: 'is' — Among the concerns is the lack... Lack = singular.
a104.are: 'are' would apply if the subject were plural, but lack is singular.

hints: Rule: Minutiae is the Latin plural of minutia, so use are.
Minutiae = fine details (plural).

a105.am: 'am' is only for I. Minutiae is plural.
a105.is: 'is' would suit minutia (singular), but minutiae is plural.
a105.are: Correct: 'are' — minutiae = plural. The minutiae are often overlooked.

hints: Rule: What is + adjective — what refers to one abstract thing (singular).
What = 'the thing that' — singular.

a106.am: 'am' is only for I. What is third person.
a106.is: Correct: 'is' — What is conspicuously absent... — what = singular.
a106.are: 'are' could work if what referred to multiple things, but here it's one.

hints: Rule: Premises (a building or property) is always plural.
Premises has no singular form in this meaning.

a107.am: 'am' is only for I. Premises is plural.
a107.is: 'is' is for singular. Premises is always plural.
a107.are: Correct: 'are' — premises = plural. The premises are under investigation.

hints: Rule: It is incumbent upon — formal construction with it as formal subject.
The formal subject It takes is.

a108.am: 'am' is only for I. It takes is.
a108.is: Correct: 'is' — It is incumbent upon... — formal it + adjective.
a108.are: 'are' is for plural. It is singular.
